en.extract:
The Rote boobook (Ninox rotiensis) is a species of owl in the family Strigidae. It is endemic to Rote Island in the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia.
It was first described in 1997 by Australian biologists Ronald Johnstone and J.C. Darnell from a female collected in a mist net in 1990. It is smaller than the Australian boobook, with heavily barred primaries, rump and tail. Locally common, it is known as Tuterui and Kokorok in Landu and Oelaba localities respectively on the island. Genetic and call analysis show it to be markedly divergent from the Australian populations of the Australian boobook, leading Gwee and colleagues to suggest it be reclassified as a separate species, which duly happened in 2019.

The '''Rote boobook''' ('''''Ninox rotiensis''''') is a species of [[owl]] in the family [[Strigidae]]. It is [[endemic]] to [[Rote Island]] in the Lesser Sunda Islands of [[Indonesia]].

It was first described in 1997 by Australian biologists Ronald Johnstone and J.C. Darnell from a female collected in a mist net in 1990.<ref name=johnstone96>{{cite journal |last1= Johnstone |first1= R. E. |last2= Darnell |first2= J. C. |year= 1997 |title= Description of a new subspecies of boobook owl ''Ninox novaeseelandiae'' (Gmelin) from Roti Island, Indonesia |journal= Western Australian Naturalist |volume= 21 |pages= 161–74 |url= http://www2.nrm.se/ve/birds/sape/GlobalOwlProject/Ninox/Ninox%20boobook%20rotiensis%20%28Johnstone%20%26%20Darnell%29%201997%2C%20Western%20Australian%20Naturalist%2C%2021%2C%20pages%20161-.PDF |archive-date= 2020-09-25 |access-date= 2019-01-20 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20200925172424/http://www2.nrm.se/ve/birds/sape/GlobalOwlProject/Ninox/Ninox%20boobook%20rotiensis%20%28Johnstone%20%26%20Darnell%29%201997%2C%20Western%20Australian%20Naturalist%2C%2021%2C%20pages%20161-.PDF |url-status= dead }}</ref> It is smaller than the Australian boobook, with heavily barred primaries, rump and tail.<ref name="König 2009">{{cite book|author1=König, Claus |author2=Weick, Friedhelm |author3=Becking, Jan-Hendrik |title=Owls of the World|publisher=A&C Black|date=2009|series=Helm Identification Guides|pages=457–59|isbn=978-1-4081-0884-0|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Rnz1c8olgWcC&pg=PA459}}</ref> Locally common, it is known as ''Tuterui'' and ''Kokorok'' in Landu and Oelaba localities respectively on the island.<ref>{{cite journal | title=Asian enigma. First field observations of ''Ninox (novaeseelandiae) rotiensis'' on Roti island, Lesser Sundas, Indonesia | first=Phillippe | last=Verbelen | journal=BirdingASIA | year=2010 | volume =13 | pages=85–89| url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/292608131}}</ref> Genetic and call analysis show it to be markedly divergent from the Australian populations of the Australian boobook, leading Gwee and colleagues to suggest it be reclassified as a separate species,<ref name=gwee2017>{{cite journal | title=Bioacoustic and multi-locus DNA data of ''Ninox'' owls support high incidence of extinction and recolonisation on small, low-lying islands across Wallacea  | last1=Gwee| first1= Chyi Yin | last2=Christidis| first2= Leslie | last3=Eaton| first3= James A. | last4=Norman| first4= Janette A.| last5=Trainor| first5= Colin R.| last6=Verbelen|first6= Phillippe | last7=Rheindt|first7= Frank E. | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |year= 2017 |volume=109 | pages=246–58 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2016.12.024 | pmid=28017857| bibcode=2017MolPE.109..246G}}</ref> which duly happened in 2019.
